product: La Roche-Posay Anthelios Melt-in Milk Sunscreen
title: Product We LOVE and HATE from La Roche-Posay | Doctorly Reviews
text:
I have a very good experience with this brand as a European customer. The large bottle sizes, lack of fragrance and unproblematic consistency to my skin is my favourite as it is on the dry to very dry and sensitive side. Sometimes, I wish the brand was a bit more clear with which products go onto the face vs the body as well as how thick or thin the cream is expected to be because I find it easy to make such a mistake. Compared to a brand like Redken which uses distinct colours and packaging alterations, LRP seems to have one style that almost all products follow. Right now, I am using and very satisfied with their Toleriane Dermo-Nettoyant (cleanser), Lipikar Baume AP+ Balm (moisturiser), Lipikar Gel Lavant (shower gel) and two Anthelios sunscreens. The only product I would not buy again is the Fluide Hydratant (body lotion): For my skin, the texture is too thin and watery, so it might be good for oily or combination skin but I prefer Neutrogena's Deep Moisture with a much thicker formula for that. Even for my face, I find the Fluide Hydratant too light and also slightly irritating around the eye area.
Their lipstick and exfoliating cleanser (Gommage Surfin) are decent in my experience, and unlike you, I have a good experience with their retinol serum as well which I use twice to thrice per week at maximum.
combined_text:

Now the thing is that physical filters are better than chemical ones, they last longer without reapplying, but it's hard to make zinc oxide small enough (preferably nano) for it not to leave white cast. Of course there are products like this, but they are mostly on the expensive side. So if you don't want white cast you can always go for the cheaper chemical filters. Just remember to reapply them around every 2-3 hours and watch out because sometimes they can cause acne breakouts

title: Dermatologist Reviews CeraVe Invisible Mineral SPF 50 | No White Cast?
text:
My PhD was in nanophotonics, where we study small structures (including micro/nanoparticles like the sunscreen ingredients discussed here) interacting with light. I totally agree that to say organic (chemical) sunscreens absorb and inorganic (mineral) sunscreens reflect is inaccurate. However, there is some truth behind the idea that these mineral sunscreens can reflect light while absorbing less. 

When the particles are around the same size as the wavelength of light (like with these sunscreen actives) the size and shape of the particles can have a very strong impact on how light is scattered, so it’s totally possible that you choose a transparent material and engineer it to reflect light. In general, intrinsic absorption occurs with reflection or scattering, so it takes some consideration to control those two features independently, but it’s very possible at these sizes.

text:
If anybody is struggling with the white casts in sunscreens, most chemical- (rather than mineral-) based sunscreens apply clear. 
In the US, the ingredient to look for is avobenzone, and the ingredients to avoid are zinc oxide and titanium dioxide. They’re all broad-spectrum.  
Chemical sunscreens bother some people with sensitive skin, and tinted mineral-based sunscreens can solve the white-cast issue in those. (The only problem of course being a tint rich enough for deeper skin tones)
